game_name: Star Wars: The Empire Strikes Back

danger_signals.visual_form: Imperial stormtroopers and hostile creatures rendered as sprite enemies in platformer levels
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat requiring combat or evasion

danger_signals.visual_form: Ice cave walls, hostile terrain in Hoth setting, obstructed pathways
Player sprite damage/knockback animation when struck
danger_signals.meaning: Taking damage from threats; risk of game-over state

safety_signals.visual_form: Collectable power-up sprites hidden in cave environments
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary invulnerability or enhanced combat capability; progression reward

safety_signals.visual_form: Rightward movement through side-scrolling levels; screen transitions
safety_signals.meaning: Safe advance through story sequence; escape from threat zones

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Luke Skywalker as playable protagonist; lightsaber and blaster weapons
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Hoth ice caves, rebel bases implied through level design

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Stormtrooper enemy sprites; Imperial iconography in level design
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Standard stormtroopers as primary enemy type across levels
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Imperial architecture, cave systems where Empire occupies territory

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Rebel under siege at Hoth base
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ice cave environment as primary level setting; snowy, frozen aesthetic dominates visual palette
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Luke must fight through Imperial forces occupying Hoth territory; evasion and combat required to escape

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stormtrooper enemies present throughout ice cave levels; hostile territory visually demarcated
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player encounters continuous enemy spawns and combat encounters; must fight through occupied zones

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Difficult platforming sections with hazards; limited resources communicated through sparse item placement
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: High difficulty progression; player must navigate treacherous terrain while under enemy pressure

environmental_storytelling.example: Hoth ice caves establish the film's opening planet; frozen environment communicates isolationist Rebel base narrative
Stormtroopers distributed throughout cave system visually demonstrate Imperial military presence and control

environmental_storytelling.technique: Hidden developer credits via environmental interaction
environmental_storytelling.example: Destroyable ice wall in first level containing hidden Force Power item leading to developer credits sequence; reward for environmental exploration
Code references to 'SPEEDER.SCENE' and tile set switches indicate visual transitions communicating location changes and escape sequences

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Can attack with blaster or lightsaber; demonstrates offensive capability against Imperial forces
Spawn in level spaces; attack player on sight; represent Imperial threat manifestation
Brief credits sequence triggers upon collection; indicates progression milestone and developer reward

notes: Limited visual communication data available from provided sources. TCRF source focuses on technical implementation (code fragments, sound test, developer credits location) rather than visual design language. The game communicates through conventional NES platformer visual grammar: sprite-based enemies indicating threat, environmental hazard tiles, collectible power-ups for safety/advancement. Faction identity relies on film franchise recognition (Luke=Rebellion, stormtroopers=Empire) rather than novel in-game visual branding systems. World-state communication appears straightforward: Hoth setting + Imperial enemy presence = rebel under siege scenario. Environmental storytelling limited to level layout and hidden item placement rewarding exploration.
sources_used: tcrf - Star Wars: The Empire Strikes Back (NES) - The Cutting Room Floor
